The static nature of experience limits the adaptability and effectiveness of these agents when confronted with new and evolving software development challenges.
Without continuous learning, agents cannot improve their performance over time or effectively manage growing experience pools.
results: The Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework improved software quality by up to 11% compared to existing baselines like ECL, GPT-Engineer, MetaGPT, and ChatDev when tested on the SRDD dataset.
Efficiency metrics (review and test) consistently improved over subsequent task batches, demonstrating the effectiveness of iterative refinement for long-term agent improvement.

The experience elimination mechanism successfully maintained or surpassed quality metrics while reducing the experience pool size to 11.54% of its original, demonstrating improved resource efficiency.
solution: The Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework enables LLM-based software development agents to continually refine their experiences, shifting from static to dynamic experience paradigms.
The framework employs multi-agent collaboration (instructive and responsive agents) to generate software solutions, along with task batching to manage experience propagation.
It acquires 'shortcuts' (efficient experiences) from agent communication chains using a self-instruction mechanism and utilizes these experiences via retrieval during task execution.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University, Dalian University of Technology, Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications, and Siemens propose an Iterative Experience Refinement (IER) framework, allowing LLM-based software development agents to continually learn and improve by dynamically refining their experience pools. This approach leads to up to 11% relative improvement in software quality and consistent efficiency gains over baselines, while an experience elimination mechanism efficiently manages the growing experience space.

title: Iterative Experience Refinement of Software-Developing Agents

abstract: Building LLM-based agents has become increasingly important. Recent works on LLM-based agent self-evolution primarily record successful experiences as textual prompts or reflections, which cannot reliably guarantee efficient task re-execution in complex scenarios. We propose AgentFactory, a new self-evolution paradigm that preserves successful task solutions as executable subagent code rather than textual experience. Crucially, these subagents are continuously refined based on execution feedback, becoming increasingly robust and efficient as more tasks are encountered. Saved subagents are pure Python code with standardized documentation, enabling portability across any Python-capable system. We demonstrate that AgentFactory enables continuous capability accumulation: its library of executable subagents grows and improves over time, progressively reducing the effort required for similar tasks without manual intervention. Our implementation is open-sourced at this https URL, and our demonstration video is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Preserving successful task solutions as executable code offers a more precise, robust, and reliable mechanism for task re-execution compared to relying on textual descriptions of experience.
Autonomous code modification driven by execution feedback allows subagents to incrementally become more general-purpose and resilient to variations and edge cases.

The design choice to store subagents as pure Python code with standardized documentation (`SKILL.md`) facilitates portability and interoperability, enabling their deployment across diverse AI frameworks.
originalProblem: Existing self-evolving LLM agents primarily record successful experiences as textual prompts or reflections, which are often inefficient and unreliable for guaranteeing robust task re-execution.
Current LLM agent frameworks generally treat agent behavior as static, lacking mechanisms to systematically preserve and utilize knowledge gained from task execution for continuous improvement.
There is a need for robust, reusable, and adaptable solutions for routine procedural tasks that can be autonomously refined over time and are portable across different AI systems.
results: A subagent for README generation demonstrated self-evolution by autonomously replacing a fragile path parsing method with a robust regex-based extraction, improving error handling.

Saved subagents like "Audio Transcriber" and "Document Creator" were successfully migrated to an external system (Claude Code), which learned to use them by interpreting their code and documentation for new complex tasks.

Quantitative evaluation showed AgentFactory significantly reduced orchestration token consumption for new tasks using saved subagents, achieving a 34% reduction for Claude Opus 4.6 (4324 to 2971 tokens) and a 58% reduction for Claude Sonnet 4.6 (9199 to 3862 tokens) compared to initial runs.
solution: AgentFactory implements a three-phase lifecycle (Install, Self-Evolve, Deploy) orchestrated by a Meta-Agent that interacts with a Skill System and a Workspace Manager.
Subagents are constructed, refined, and stored as pure, executable Python code, encapsulating successful task-solving patterns and allowing for autonomous modification based on execution feedback.
The framework employs a unified skill interface comprising Meta Skills (orchestration), Tool Skills (static external tools), and Subagent Skills (dynamically created and refined modules).
paper_summary.summary: AgentFactory is a self-evolving framework enabling large language model (LLM) agents to continuously accumulate and improve task-solving capabilities by creating, refining, and reusing executable subagent code. The framework demonstrated reduced orchestration token consumption by up to 58% when reusing saved subagents for new tasks compared to solving from scratch.

title: AgentFactory: A Self-Evolving Framework Through Executable Subagent Accumulation and Reuse

abstract: While large language models (LLMs) have advanced the development of general-purpose agents, achieving robust generalization to unseen tasks remains a significant challenge. Current approaches typically rely on either fine-tuning or training-free memory-augmented generation using retrieved experience; yet both have limitations: fine-tuning often fails to extrapolate to new tasks, while experience retrieval often underperforms compared to supervised baselines. In this work, we propose to combine these approaches and systematically study how to train retrieval-augmented LLM agents to effectively leverage retrieved trajectories in-context. First, we establish a robust supervised fine-tuning (SFT) recipe using LoRA that outperforms several state-of-the-art agent training pipelines. Second, we provide a detailed analysis of key design choices for experience retrieval, identifying optimal strategies for storage, querying, and trajectory selection. Finally, we propose a pipeline that integrates experience retrieval into the fine-tuning process. Our results demonstrate that this combined approach significantly improves generalization to unseen tasks, providing a scalable and effective framework for building agents that learn to learn from experience.

keyInsights: Training LLM agents with retrieved experience (ExpRAG-LoRA) teaches the model to inherently leverage such context, leading to superior out-of-distribution generalization compared to applying fine-tuning or retrieval in isolation.
Applying retrieval only at inference time to a standard LoRA-trained model also improves out-of-distribution performance compared to bare LoRA, but ExpRAG-LoRA generally yields higher success rates.

LLM agent fine-tuning exhibits "emergent delayed downstream generalization," where out-of-distribution performance continues to improve for many epochs, even after validation loss starts to increase, suggesting prolonged training is beneficial for robustness.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents struggle with robust generalization to tasks not encountered during training, often due to distribution shifts and compounding errors.
Fine-tuned LLM agents perform well on in-distribution tasks but often experience performance collapse on out-of-distribution tasks.
Training-free, memory-augmented LLM agents using retrieved experience for in-context information typically underperform supervised baselines, despite their inference-time adaptiveness.
results: ExpRAG-LoRA improved out-of-distribution success on ALFWorld hard tasks for Ministral 3-8B from 34.4% (LoRA) to 88.5%, significantly outperforming LoRA with inference-time retrieval (67.2%).

Inference-only experience retrieval substantially increased all-task success rates over zero-shot baselines (e.g., Ministral 3-8B on ALFWorld from 4.48% to 64.18%, and ScienceWorld from 10.40% to 35.24%).
The findings were consistent across all evaluated backbone models (Ministral 3-8B, Gemma 3-4B, Qwen 2.5-7B, Qwen 2.5-7B-1M) and both ALFWorld and ScienceWorld benchmarks.
solution: Developed ExpRAG-LoRA, a method that integrates episodic experience retrieval directly into the parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) process for LLM agents.
Constructed an offline experience bank of expert interaction trajectories, formatted as multi-turn chats, with key embeddings for efficient retrieval.

Evaluated both inference-only experience retrieval and the proposed ExpRAG-LoRA across various backbone models and environments like ALFWorld and ScienceWorld, specifically assessing generalization to unseen tasks.
paper_summary.summary: NAVER LABS Europe developed ExpRAG-LoRA, a framework that enables Large Language Model agents to robustly generalize to novel tasks by integrating experience retrieval directly into the fine-tuning process. This approach significantly improves out-of-distribution success rates compared to models trained without retrieval or those relying solely on inference-time retrieval.

title: Retrieval-Augmented LLM Agents: Learning to Learn from Experience

abstract: Anthropic proposes the concept of skills for LLM agents to tackle multi-step professional tasks that simple tool invocations cannot address. A tool is a single, self-contained function, whereas a skill is a structured bundle of interdependent multi-file artifacts. Currently, skill generation is not only label-intensive due to manual authoring, but also may suffer from human--machine cognitive misalignment, which can lead to degraded agent performance, as evidenced by evaluations on SkillsBench. Therefore, we aim to enable agents to autonomously generate skills. However, existing self-evolving methods designed for tools cannot be directly applied to skills due to their increased complexity. To address these issues, we propose EvoSkills, a self-evolving skills framework that enables agents to autonomously construct complex, multi-file skill packages. Specifically, EvoSkills couples a Skill Generator that iteratively refines skills with a Surrogate Verifier that co-evolves to provide informative and actionable feedback without access to ground-truth test content. On SkillsBench, EvoSkills achieves the highest pass rate among five baselines on both Claude Code and Codex, and also exhibits strong generalization capabilities to six additional LLMs.

keyInsights: LLM agents can autonomously generate more effective and reliable skills than human experts, as these self-evolved skills better align with the agent's intrinsic reasoning and execution patterns.
A co-evolutionary framework, especially with an information-isolated surrogate verifier, provides crucial, actionable feedback for skill refinement without requiring direct ground-truth supervision.
Self-evolved skills demonstrate strong cross-model transferability, suggesting they encode reusable task structures rather than model-specific artifacts, making them broadly portable.
originalProblem: Manual human authoring of 'agent skills' is labor-intensive, difficult to scale, and often leads to inconsistent performance due to 'human-machine cognitive misalignment'.
Existing self-evolving methods for LLM agents focus primarily on atomic tools or function APIs, lacking the capability to generate and refine complex, structured, multi-file skill packages.
Many self-evolution approaches require ground-truth supervision for failure diagnosis, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios where such detailed feedback is unavailable.
results: EvoSkills achieved a 71.1% pass rate on SkillsBench with Claude Opus 4.6, outperforming the no-skill baseline (30.6%) by +40.5 percentage points and human-curated skills (53.5%) by +17.6 percentage points.
Ablation studies confirmed that removing the surrogate verifier led to a substantial drop in pass rate by -30.0 percentage points, highlighting its critical role in targeted repairs.
Skills evolved by Claude Opus 4.6 showed strong transferability across six other LLM models, improving their respective no-skill baselines by +36 to +44 percentage points.

Self-evolved skills outperformed human-curated skills in 9 out of 11 professional domains, including significant gains in Finance (+56.9 pp) and Cybersecurity (+23.2 pp), and corrected degraded performance in Natural Science.
solution: The EvoSkills framework introduces a co-evolutionary approach, employing a Skill Generator and an information-isolated Surrogate Verifier that iteratively refine skills and their verification mechanisms.
The Skill Generator autonomously generates and refines multi-file skill bundles by processing task instructions and structured failure diagnostics from the verifier.

The Surrogate Verifier independently generates and refines a suite of deterministic test assertions, providing dense, actionable feedback and escalating its tests when ground-truth failures occur despite surrogate success.
paper_summary.summary: EvoSkills enables large language model agents to autonomously generate and iteratively refine complex, multi-file skill packages through a co-evolutionary verification framework. This approach yields skills that surpass human-authored versions and exhibit strong transferability across different LLM architectures, effectively bridging the cognitive gap between human-designed workflows and agent execution.

title: EvoSkills: Self-Evolving Agent Skills via Co-Evolutionary Verification

abstract: LLM-driven agents demonstrate strong performance in sequential decision-making but often rely on on-the-fly reasoning, re-deriving solutions even in recurring scenarios. This insufficient experience reuse leads to computational redundancy and execution instability. To bridge this gap, we propose ProcMEM, a framework that enables agents to autonomously learn procedural memory from interaction experiences without parameter updates. By formalizing a Skill-MDP, ProcMEM transforms passive episodic narratives into executable Skills defined by activation, execution, and termination conditions to ensure executability. To achieve reliable reusability without capability degradation, we introduce Non-Parametric PPO, which leverages semantic gradients for high-quality candidate generation and a PPO Gate for robust Skill verification. Through score-based maintenance, ProcMEM sustains compact, high-quality procedural memory. Experimental results across in-domain, cross-task, and cross-agent scenarios demonstrate that ProcMEM achieves superior reuse rates and significant performance gains with extreme memory compression. Visualized evolutionary trajectories and Skill distributions further reveal how ProcMEM transparently accumulates, refines, and reuses procedural knowledge to facilitate long-term autonomy.

keyInsights: Procedural memory, structured as executable "Skills," offers a significantly more efficient and compact method for LLM agents to leverage past experience compared to passive episodic memory.

Autonomous learning and refinement of complex behaviors in LLM agents are achievable through non-parametric optimization, effectively avoiding the high costs and risks of catastrophic forgetting associated with parametric fine-tuning.
Combining natural-language semantic gradients with PPO-style trust-region verification provides a robust mechanism to guide the evolution of high-quality, reusable procedural knowledge in LLM agents.
originalProblem: LLM agents typically rely on *de novo* reasoning for each scenario, even recurring ones, leading to computational redundancy, increased inference costs, and instability in long-horizon tasks.
Existing memory-augmented LLM agents largely use *episodic memory* (passive narratives), which still requires significant on-the-fly interpretation and consumes valuable context window space.

Autonomously learning reusable procedural memory in LLM agents faces challenges in transforming passive narratives into executable units, ensuring reliable invocation and consistent performance gains, and optimizing without updating LLM parameters.
results: ProcMEM demonstrated superior experience reuse rates (e.g., 0.925 in-domain, 0.825 cross-task) and maintained high performance (up to 0.90 success rate in ALFWorld) compared to baselines.

The framework achieved extreme memory compression, storing Skills with only 816 total tokens, which is orders of magnitude less than episodic memory baselines (e.g., RAG at 116,527 tokens), with minimal execution overhead.

Ablation studies confirmed that all core components of ProcMEM (Skills, Non-Parametric PPO, Semantic Gradients, PPO Gate, and Score-based Maintenance) are critical for its effectiveness, reuse rate, and stability.
solution: ProcMEM formalizes reusable procedural units as "Skills" (activation, execution, termination conditions) within a Skill-augmented Markov Decision Process (Skill-MDP) for hierarchical decision-making.

It introduces Non-Parametric PPO for skill evolution, employing semantic gradients (natural-language refinement suggestions via hindsight attribution) combined with a PPO-style trust-region verification (PPO Gate) to refine and validate skill candidates.

An online score-based mechanism maintains the Skill pool by tracking utility, pruning underperforming or semantically redundant skills, and ensuring a compact, high-quality procedural memory over time.
paper_summary.summary: ProcMEM, a framework developed by researchers including those from Chinese Academy of Sciences and UCL, enables LLM agents to learn and reuse "Skills" as procedural memory from experience using a non-parametric Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) mechanism. It achieves superior experience reuse rates, extreme memory compression, and improved performance across diverse tasks and agents, offering a more efficient alternative to traditional episodic memory or parametric fine-tuning for complex decision-making.

title: ProcMEM: Learning Reusable Procedural Memory from Experience via Non-Parametric PPO for LLM Agents

abstract: Large Language Model agents demonstrate potential in solving real-world problems via tools, yet generalist intelligence is bottlenecked by scarce high-quality, long-horizon data. Existing methods collect privacy-constrained API logs or generate scripted interactions lacking diversity, which struggle to produce data requisite for scaling capabilities. We propose AgentSkiller, a fully automated framework synthesizing multi-turn interaction data across realistic, semantically linked domains. It employs a DAG-based architecture with explicit state transitions to ensure determinism and recoverability. The pipeline builds a domain ontology and Person-Centric Entity Graph, defines tool interfaces via Service Blueprints for Model Context Protocol servers, and populates environments with consistent databases and strict Domain Policies. A cross-domain fusion mechanism links services to simulate complex tasks. Finally, the pipeline creates user tasks by verifying solution paths, filtering via execution-based validation, and generating queries using a Persona-based Simulator for automated rollout. This produces reliable environments with clear state changes. To demonstrate effectiveness, we synthesized $\approx$ 11K interaction samples; experimental results indicate that models trained on this dataset achieve significant improvements on function calling over baselines, particularly in larger parameter regimes.

keyInsights: Data quality, structural depth, and semantic integration across domains are more critical determinants of generalist agent performance than merely increasing model parameter count.

A "Generate-Implement-Validate" methodology, coupled with a dual-model architecture and state-machine orchestration, effectively mitigates LLM stochasticity to produce reliable, high-fidelity synthetic data.
Scaling generalist agent intelligence necessitates a focus on synthesizing complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks that seamlessly integrate information and actions across disparate service domains.
originalProblem: Scarcity of high-quality, long-horizon, multi-turn, and cross-domain interaction data needed to train generalist Large Language Model (LLM) agents.
Existing data generation methods (real-world logs, scripted synthesis) are constrained by privacy, limited scenario coverage, and lack of scalability or semantic complexity.
Many synthetic environments fail to capture realistic, complex interoperability required for agents to reason across multiple, interconnected service domains.
results: Synthesized a large-scale dataset of approximately 11,522 high-fidelity, multi-turn interaction samples, with 5,581 representing complex cross-domain scenarios involving 15-20 turns.

The AgentSkiller-14B model, trained on this dataset, achieved a 79.1% overall score on the 𝜏²-bench, outperforming GPT-o3 (68.4%) and Claude-Sonnet-4 (56.8%), and scored 78.0% on ACEBench-Agent, surpassing Gemini-2.5-pro by 14.6%.

Performance consistently improved when training with the full cross-domain dataset compared to single-domain data, demonstrating the critical value of diverse, interleaved, cross-domain training trajectories for generalist agents.
solution: A fully automated, scalable framework named AgentSkiller that synthesizes high-fidelity, multi-turn interaction data across semantically linked domains.
Employs a dual-model architecture (Textual LLM for high-level semantics, Coding LLM for precise code generation) and state-machine orchestration for deterministic and recoverable data synthesis.
Integrates a rigorous test-driven self-correction mechanism to ensure functional robustness and policy compliance in generated environments and interaction trajectories.
paper_summary.summary: AgentSkiller introduces a fully automated framework for generating high-fidelity, multi-turn, cross-domain interaction data to train generalist AI agents. Models trained on this synthesized dataset, like the 14B-parameter AgentSkiller, achieve a 79.1% overall score on the 𝜏²-bench, surpassing larger proprietary models and indicating the importance of data quality over model size.

title: AgentSkiller: Scaling Generalist Agent Intelligence through Semantically Integrated Cross-Domain Data Synthesis

abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning (RL) can benefit substantially from reusable experience, yet existing skill-based methods mainly extract trajectory-level guidance and often lack principled mechanisms for maintaining an evolving skill memory. We propose D2Skill, a dynamic dual-granularity skill bank for agentic RL that organizes reusable experience into task skills for high-level guidance and step skills for fine-grained decision support and error correction. D2Skill jointly trains the policy and skill bank through paired baseline and skill-injected rollouts under the same policy, using their performance gap to derive hindsight utility signals for both skill updating and policy optimization. Built entirely from training-time experience, the skill bank is continuously expanded through reflection and maintained with utility-aware retrieval and pruning. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 show that D2Skill consistently improves success rates over skill-free baselines by 10-20 points. Further ablations and analyses show that both dual-granularity skill modeling and dynamic skill maintenance are critical to these gains, while the learned skills exhibit higher utility, transfer across evaluation settings, and introduce only modest training overhead.

keyInsights: Both high-level task guidance and fine-grained step-level support are crucial for optimal agent performance, as evidenced by the performance degradation when either skill granularity is ablated.
Dynamic management of the skill bank, including principled pruning based on utility, is essential to prevent performance degradation from skill accumulation and maintain retrieval efficiency.
Large Language Models (LLMs) can be highly effective as 'reflectors' for abstracting reusable skills from trajectories, even if they are not strong standalone agents for direct action generation.
originalProblem: Existing skill-based agentic RL methods primarily derive skills from complete trajectories, offering coarse-grained guidance but limiting fine-grained error correction at individual interaction steps.
The continuous expansion of skill banks in agentic RL leads to scalability issues, increased retrieval latency, and the accumulation of redundant or ineffective skills without principled management.
Challenges in credit assignment due to partial observability and sparse rewards hinder efficient learning and knowledge transfer in long-horizon, complex decision-making tasks.
results: D2Skill improved success rates by 10–20 percentage points over skill-free baselines on ALFWORLD and WEBSHOP benchmarks (e.g., 90.6% on ALFWORLD vs. 75.0% for GRPO).
The framework achieved comparable or faster training times (e.g., 25.6 hours on ALFWORLD vs. 49.2 hours for SkillRL) while delivering superior performance.
Ablation studies confirmed that dual-granularity skills, skill management, and the utility mechanism individually contribute to the overall performance gains.
solution: D2Skill introduces a dual-granularity skill bank, distinguishing between high-level 'task skills' for strategic guidance and fine-grained 'step skills' for local error correction during interactions.
A dynamic skill bank maintenance system is implemented, utilizing reflection-driven generation and utility-aware retrieval and pruning mechanisms to keep the skill bank compact and effective.

A joint training paradigm is employed where policy and skill bank co-evolve, using performance gaps between skill-injected and baseline rollouts to generate hindsight utility signals for both skill updates and policy optimization.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the Institute of Automation, Chinese Academy of Sciences, developed D2Skill, a framework for agentic reinforcement learning that employs a dynamic, dual-granularity skill bank to manage reusable knowledge. This system continuously generates and prunes both task-level and step-level skills, outperforming skill-free baselines by 10-20 points in success rate on ALFWORLD and WEBSHOP benchmarks and achieving up to 1.7 times faster training compared to prior skill-augmented methods.

title: Dynamic Dual-Granularity Skill Bank for Agentic RL

abstract: We study continual skill acquisition in open-ended embodied environments where an agent must construct, refine, and reuse an expanding library of executable skills. We introduce the Programmatic Skill Network (PSN), a framework in which skills are executable symbolic programs forming a compositional network that evolves through experience. PSN defines three core mechanisms instantiated via large language models: (1)REFLECT for structured fault localization over skill compositions, (2) progressive optimization with maturity-aware update gating that stabilizes reliable skills while maintaining plasticity for uncertain ones, and (3) canonical structural refactoring under rollback validation that maintains network compactness. We further show that PSN's learning dynamics exhibit structural parallels to neural network training. Experiments on MineDojo and Crafter demonstrate robust skill reuse, rapid adaptation, and strong generalization across open-ended task distributions.\footnote{We plan to open-source the code.

keyInsights: Learning dynamics in symbolic systems, such as PSN, exhibit structural parallels to neural network training, with fault localization akin to backpropagation and refactoring to neural architecture search.

A multi-scale learning dynamic, integrating fast behavioral repair, intermediate stabilization, and slow structural reorganization, is critical for coherent and stable continual learning in embodied agents.

Explicit programmatic skill representation allows for inspectable computational graphs, enabling principled credit assignment and dynamic structural optimization for improved interpretability and robustness.
originalProblem: Embodied agents struggle to continually acquire and refine skills in dynamic, open-ended environments without suffering from catastrophic forgetting.
Existing LLM-based agents often rely on flat skill libraries or static graphs, lacking mechanisms for continuous improvement or robust credit assignment over hierarchical compositions.
Agents lack unified frameworks to pinpoint errors in symbolic programs, repair them, and dynamically reorganize skill structures for efficiency and reuse.
results: PSN significantly outperformed baselines (ReAct, Reflexion, AutoGPT, Voyager) in Minecraft tech tree mastery, unlocking higher-tier items faster, with fewer iterations, and lower variance.

It achieved higher cumulative rewards in the Crafter environment, demonstrating sustained progress and robust survival, and showed strong resistance to catastrophic forgetting, maintaining skill retention as new skills were acquired.

Ablation studies confirmed the criticality of PSN's end-to-end optimizer for stable scaling, maturity-aware update gating for preventing oscillatory behavior, and structural refactoring for managing network growth and promoting compositional generalization.
solution: An online framework, Programmatic Skill Networks (PSN), builds, optimizes, and refactors a library of executable symbolic skills, structured as a directed graph.
It employs a REFLECT operator for trace-based credit assignment, diagnosing failures in compositional skill executions and proposing symbolic edits.
Maturity-aware update gating stabilizes reliable skills, while canonical structural refactoring maintains network compactness and enhances reuse, both validated by rollback mechanisms.
paper_summary.summary: The research introduces Programmatic Skill Networks (PSN), an online framework enabling embodied agents to continually learn, refine, and reuse executable skills in open-ended environments. PSN demonstrates superior performance in mastering complex tech trees like Minecraft and sustains higher cumulative rewards in Crafter, while effectively mitigating catastrophic forgetting and promoting compositional generalization.

title: Evolving Programmatic Skill Networks

abstract: Large Language Model agents are reshaping the industrial landscape. However, most practical agents remain human-designed because tasks differ widely, making them labor-intensive to build. This situation poses a central question: can we automatically create and adapt domain agents in the wild? While several recent approaches have sought to automate agent creation, they typically treat agent generation as a black-box procedure and rely solely on final performance metrics to guide the process. Such strategies overlook critical evidence explaining why an agent succeeds or fails, and often require high computational costs. To address these limitations, we propose ReCreate, an experience-driven framework for the automatic creation of domain agents. ReCreate systematically leverages agent interaction histories, which provide rich concrete signals on both the causes of success or failure and the avenues for improvement. Specifically, we introduce an agent-as-optimizer paradigm that effectively learns from experience via three key components: (i) an experience storage and retrieval mechanism for on-demand inspection; (ii) a reasoning-creating synergy pipeline that maps execution experience into scaffold edits; and (iii) hierarchical updates that abstract instance-level details into reusable domain patterns. In experiments across diverse domains, ReCreate consistently outperforms human-designed agents and existing automated agent generation methods, even when starting from minimal seed scaffolds.

keyInsights: Leveraging rich interaction experience provides transparent, 'white-box' diagnostic signals that are critical for understanding agent failures and successes, leading to more efficient and targeted scaffold improvements than metric-only feedback.

An 'agent-as-optimizer' paradigm, where an LLM reasons, inspects, and creates scaffold components, can effectively automate the complex design and debugging of agent systems, mimicking human expert engineering processes.

Synthesizing instance-level fixes into generalized domain patterns via hierarchical updates is essential for preventing overfitting and ensuring that created agents are robust and transferable across different tasks within a domain.
originalProblem: The creation of LLM agents for complex tasks heavily relies on labor-intensive, human-designed 'scaffolds,' which are difficult and costly to scale across various domains.

Existing automated agent generation methods treat optimization as a black-box problem, using only performance metrics that lack diagnostic information, leading to inefficient and computationally expensive trial-and-error processes.
Current self-evolving methods primarily refine pre-existing agent scaffolds for instance-level success, failing to build agents from scratch or ensure domain-level generalization.
results: ReCreate consistently outperforms human-designed agents and existing automated generation methods, achieving an average performance improvement of over 5% across 13 benchmarks in software engineering, data science, mathematics, and digital assistance domains.

The framework demonstrates significant cost-effectiveness, reducing agent generation costs by approximately 36% to 82% compared to black-box methods like ADAS, due to faster convergence from diagnostic evidence.

Ablation studies confirm that full interaction trajectories are crucial for performance, and the effectiveness of ReCreate is highly dependent on the reasoning capabilities of the underlying optimizer LLM, with frontier models like Claude-4.5-opus enabling superior results.
solution: ReCreate frames domain agent creation as an 'experience-driven,' white-box debugging process, where a dedicated 'ReCreate-Agent' LLM acts as an optimizer for task agent scaffolds.

It employs an 'Experience Storage and Retrieval' mechanism to manage vast interaction data, allowing the ReCreate-Agent to perform on-demand inspection and retrieve relevant evidence from full trajectories, execution logs, and environment states.

A 'Reasoning–Creation Synergy Pipeline' coupled with a 'Hierarchical Local-to-Domain Updates' mechanism enables the ReCreate-Agent to infer specific scaffold modifications from evidence, aggregate instance-level insights into generalized domain patterns, and prevent overfitting.
paper_summary.summary: ReCreate is a framework that enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically create and adapt domain agents by systematically leveraging rich interaction experiences, rather than relying on human-designed scaffolds. The framework achieves superior performance, improving overall scores by over 5% compared to strong baselines, and reduces agent generation costs by 36% to 82% across diverse domains.

title: ReCreate: Reasoning and Creating Domain Agents Driven by Experience

abstract:
Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown promise in tackling
complex tasks by interacting dynamically with the environment. Existing work
primarily focuses on behavior cloning from expert demonstrations or preference
learning through exploratory trajectory sampling. However, these methods often
struggle to address long-horizon tasks, where suboptimal actions accumulate
step by step, causing agents to deviate from correct task trajectories. To
address this, we highlight the importance of timely calibration and the need to
automatically construct calibration trajectories for training agents. We
propose Step-Level Trajectory Calibration (STeCa), a novel framework for LLM
agent learning. Specifically, STeCa identifies suboptimal actions through a
step-level reward comparison during exploration. It constructs calibrated
trajectories using LLM-driven reflection, enabling agents to learn from
improved decision-making processes. We finally leverage these calibrated
trajectories with successful trajectories for reinforced training. Extensive
experiments demonstrate that STeCa significantly outperforms existing methods.
Further analysis highlights that timely calibration enables agents to complete
tasks with greater robustness. Our code and data are available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Timely, step-level calibration is essential for mitigating the accumulation of suboptimal actions in long-horizon tasks, leading to improved task success.
Monte Carlo sampling can effectively estimate step-level rewards for real-time deviation detection, supported by the empirical Markov property in agentic tasks.
Generating high-quality, externally-derived reflective thoughts significantly enhances an agent's self-correction capabilities compared to self-generated reflections.
originalProblem: LLM agents performing long-horizon tasks accumulate suboptimal actions, causing deviations and task failure.
Current learning paradigms detect errors primarily at the terminal state, failing to provide timely, step-level corrections.
A lack of automated methods for constructing step-level calibration data hinders agents' ability to self-correct during task execution.
results:
STeCa achieved an average final reward of 70.9% on VirtualHome and ALFWorld, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art IPR by 3.4% and E

2CL by 4.0%.
It demonstrated consistent performance improvements across different base LLMs (Mistral-7B, Llama-3-8B-Instruct) and improved generalization to unseen tasks by 17.1% on Mistral-7B.
The framework exhibited enhanced robustness against deviated actions, showing minimal performance degradation and generating actions with higher affordance rates compared to baselines.
solution: A supervised fine-tuning warm-up phase initializes the LLM agent on expert ReAct-style trajectories.
Step-level deviation detection identifies suboptimal actions in real-time using Monte Carlo sampled step-level rewards, and an external LLM generates reflective thoughts to calibrate these actions.
Reinforced training integrates these calibrated trajectories with explored successful and expert sub-trajectories, employing a reward mechanism based on normalized Dynamic Time Warping (nDTW).
paper_summary.summary: The STeCa framework introduces step-level trajectory calibration for LLM agents, enabling real-time self-correction in long-horizon tasks. It employs Monte Carlo sampling to detect suboptimal actions and uses a large language model (GPT-4o) to generate reflective thoughts, constructing calibrated training data. This approach achieved an average final reward of 70.9% on VirtualHome and ALFWorld, surpassing prior methods like IPR by 3.4%, and showed enhanced robustness against deviated actions.

title: STeCa: Step-level Trajectory Calibration for LLM Agent Learning

abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the development of autonomous agents capable of complex reasoning and multi-step problem solving. However, these agents struggle to adapt to specialized environments and do not leverage past interactions, approaching each new task from scratch regardless of their accumulated experience. We introduce Experiential Reflective Learning (ERL), a simple self-improvement framework that enables rapid environment adaptation through experiential learning. ERL reflects on task trajectories and outcomes to generate heuristics, capturing actionable lessons that transfer across tasks. At test time, relevant heuristics are retrieved based on the current task and injected into the agent's context to guide execution. On the Gaia2 benchmark, ERL improves success rate by 7.8% over a ReAct baseline, with large gains in task completion reliability, and outperforms prior experiential learning methods. Through systematic ablations, we find that selective retrieval is essential and that heuristics provide more transferable abstractions than few-shot trajectory prompting. These results demonstrate that reflecting on single-attempt experiences to extract transferable heuristics enables effective agent self-improvement.
title: Experiential Reflective Learning for Self-Improving LLM Agents

abstract: Open-world embodied agents must solve long-horizon tasks where the main bottleneck is not single-step planning quality but how interaction experience is organized and evolved. To this end, we present Steve-Evolving, a non-parametric self-evolving framework that tightly couples fine-grained execution diagnosis with dual-track knowledge distillation in a closed loop. The method follows three phases: Experience Anchoring, Experience Distillation, and Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control. In detail, Experience Anchoring solidifies each subgoal attempt into a structured experience tuple with a fixed schema (pre-state, action, diagnosis-result, and post-state) and organizes it in a three-tier experience space with multi-dimensional indices (e.g., condition signatures, spatial hashing, and semantic tags) plus rolling summarization for efficient and auditable recall. To ensure sufficient information density for attribution, the execution layer provides compositional diagnosis signals beyond binary outcomes, including state-difference summaries, enumerated failure causes, continuous indicators, and stagnation/loop detection. Moreover, successful trajectories of Experience Distillation are generalized into reusable skills with explicit preconditions and verification criteria, while failures are distilled into executable guardrails that capture root causes and forbid risky operations at both subgoal and task granularities. Besides, Knowledge-Driven Closed-Loop Control retrieved skills and guardrails are injected into an LLM planner, and diagnosis-triggered local replanning updates the active constraints online, forming a continual evolution process without any model parameter updates. Experiments on the long-horizon suite of Minecraft MCU demonstrate consistent improvements over static-retrieval baselines.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: The primary bottleneck for embodied agents in complex, long-horizon tasks is not single-step planning but the inefficient organization and evolution of accumulated interaction experience.
Analogous to human expertise, agent capability significantly improves by transforming raw interaction data into structured, generalizable knowledge such as 'skills' and 'guardrails.'

Fine-grained diagnostic signals from the execution layer are essential for accurately attributing failure root causes, which then enables the systematic distillation of precise, computable error-avoidance constraints.
originalProblem: Current embodied agents face difficulties with long-horizon, compound tasks in open-world environments due to inefficient organization and evolution of interaction experience.

Existing methods primarily accumulate raw interaction instances or rely on verbal self-reflection, lacking the fine-grained diagnostic signals needed to pinpoint specific environmental anomalies causing failures.
Complex failure modes in embodied environments (e.g., spatial navigation, GUI operations) make it challenging to derive precise, computable error-avoidance constraints from past interactions.
results: Steve-Evolving achieved the highest overall success rate (e.g., 53.37% with Gemini-3-pro) across five LLM backbones on the Minecraft MCU tech-tree suite, outperforming baselines like Optimus-1 and JARVIS-1.

Ablation studies confirmed that the framework's core components—knowledge visibility, guardrail distillation, and skill distillation—are critical, with disabling knowledge visibility causing the most significant performance degradation.

The approach demonstrated effective learning from both successes and failures through case studies, enabling dynamic replanning for obstacles and distilling explicit skills and guardrails from recurrent issues.
solution: Steve-Evolving introduces a closed-loop framework that first captures fine-grained diagnostic information from embodied interactions, organizing it into structured experience documents.

It then distills this raw experience into higher-level, transferable knowledge: 'skills' from successful sequences and 'guardrails' from failures, which include execution constraints and procedural guards.

This distilled knowledge is integrated into an LLM planner, where skills act as demonstrations and guardrails as negative constraints, enabling dynamic, knowledge-driven planning and local replanning during execution.
paper_summary.summary: Steve-Evolving presents a framework for embodied agents to continuously improve in open-world environments by employing fine-grained execution diagnosis and dual-track knowledge distillation. This enables autonomous accomplishment of complex, long-horizon tasks, demonstrating superior success rates across various LLM backbones in the Minecraft MCU tech-tree suite.

title: Steve-Evolving: Open-World Embodied Self-Evolution via Fine-Grained Diagnosis and Dual-Track Knowledge Distillation

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown strong potential in multi-task scenarios, owing to their ability to transfer knowledge across diverse tasks. However, existing approaches often treat prior experiences and knowledge as monolithic units, leading to inefficient and coarse-grained knowledge transfer. In this work, we propose a novel hierarchical memory architecture that enables fine-grained knowledge transfer by decoupling high-level planning memory from low-level execution memory. To construct and refine these hierarchical memories, we introduce Hierarchical Hindsight Reflection (H$^2$R), a mechanism that distills reusable and hierarchical knowledge from past agent-environment interactions. At test time, H$^2$R performs retrievals of high-level and low-level memories separately, allowing LLM-based agents to efficiently access and utilize task-relevant knowledge for new this http URL results across two benchmarks demonstrate that H$^2$R can improve generalization and decision-making performance, outperforming prior baselines such as Expel.

keyInsights: Decoupling knowledge into strategic planning and tactical execution levels enables more fine-grained and context-relevant knowledge transfer for LLM agents.
A two-tiered hindsight reflection process effectively distills reusable insights at both high-level (planning) and low-level (execution) granularities from successful and failed trajectories.
Hierarchical memory organization minimizes interference from irrelevant past experiences by specializing memory units for task-level strategies and subgoal-level mechanics.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems often treat prior experiences and knowledge as monolithic or coarse-grained units.
This leads to inefficient knowledge transfer and interference from irrelevant information when agents encounter new tasks in multi-task scenarios.
Agents struggle to selectively access and utilize only the minimally task-relevant fragments of knowledge for robust generalization.
results: H²R consistently outperformed baselines, achieving 75.9% success in AlfWorld (3.5% relative improvement over ExpeL) and 80.5% in PDDLGame (8.3% relative improvement over ExpeL).

Ablation studies demonstrated that both high-level planning memories (27.7% performance drop when removed) and low-level execution memories (19.4% performance drop when removed) are essential for the framework's full performance.
The framework's ability to decouple planning and execution knowledge was particularly beneficial in complex hierarchical planning environments like PDDLGame.
solution: H²R proposes a hierarchical memory architecture that decouples knowledge into high-level planning memory and low-level execution memory.
It introduces a Hierarchical Hindsight Reflection (H²R) mechanism to distill reusable, hierarchical knowledge from agent-environment interactions into these two memory components.
An LLM-based Planner leverages high-level memories for strategic task decomposition, while an Executor uses low-level memories for fine-grained action execution on specific subgoals.
paper_summary.summary: The H²R framework introduces a hierarchical memory structure and a two-tiered reflection mechanism for multi-task LLM agents, facilitating more effective and fine-grained knowledge transfer. This approach improves agent generalization and decision-making, achieving 75.9% success in AlfWorld and 80.5% in PDDLGame, surpassing strong baselines like ExpeL.

title: H$^2$R: Hierarchical Hindsight Reflection for Multi-Task LLM Agents

abstract: While skill-centric approaches leverage foundation models to enhance generalization in compositional tasks, they often rely on fixed skill libraries, limiting adaptability to new tasks without manual intervention. To address this, we propose Uni-Skill, a Unified Skill-centric framework that supports skill-aware planning and facilitates automatic skill evolution. Unlike prior methods that restrict planning to predefined skills, Uni-Skill requests for new skill implementations when existing ones are insufficient, ensuring adaptable planning with self-augmented skill library. To support automatic implementation of diverse skills requested by the planning module, we construct SkillFolder, a VerbNet-inspired repository derived from large-scale unstructured robotic videos. SkillFolder introduces a hierarchical skill taxonomy that captures diverse skill descriptions at multiple levels of abstraction. By populating this taxonomy with large-scale, automatically annotated demonstrations, Uni-Skill shifts the paradigm of skill acquisition from inefficient manual annotation to efficient offline structural retrieval. Retrieved examples provide semantic supervision over behavior patterns and fine-grained references for spatial trajectories, enabling few-shot skill inference without deployment-time demonstrations. Comprehensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings verify the state-of-the-art performance of Uni-Skill over existing VLM-based skill-centric approaches, highlighting its advanced reasoning capabilities and strong zero-shot generalization across a wide range of novel tasks.

keyInsights: Robotic systems can dynamically identify skill gaps and autonomously generate high-level descriptions for necessary new skills.
Unstructured robotic videos can be transformed into actionable skill knowledge through automated annotation and a VerbNet-inspired hierarchical organization (SkillFolder).
Few-shot skill implementation can be achieved by extracting and leveraging fine-grained spatial trajectories and high-level semantic constraints from retrieved skill-centric demonstrations.
originalProblem: Current skill-centric robotic manipulation approaches rely on fixed skill libraries, leading to failures on tasks requiring unforeseen capabilities.
Acquiring new robotic skills is inefficient and unscalable, typically requiring manual human supervision or waypoint annotations.
Abundant unstructured robotic video data cannot be directly leveraged for expanding skill libraries due due to a lack of annotations and structure.
results: Achieved an average success rate of 0.41 on novel tasks in simulation (RLBench), outperforming MOKA (0.10) by 31.0% in zero-shot success and CaP (0.01).
Demonstrated an average success rate of 0.73 in real-world experiments on a Franka Emika robot arm, surpassing MOKA (0.39) with improvements of 20.0% on long-horizon tasks and 34.0% in unseen skills.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of the skill updating mechanism and the referential guidance (semantic constraints and spatial trajectories) from SkillFolder examples for robust generalization.
solution: Introduces Uni-Skill, a Unified Skill-centric framework that integrates Skill-Aware Planning and Automatic Skill Evolution.
Skill-Aware Planning uses a VLM-based discriminator to detect skill gaps, a generator to synthesize new skill descriptions, and a planner to create executable policy code.

Automatic Skill Evolution employs a VLM pipeline for annotating unstructured videos, organizes skills into a hierarchical SkillFolder, and facilitates few-shot skill implementation using retrieved examples and constrained visual prompting.
paper_summary.summary: The Uni-Skill framework enables robots to automatically acquire and implement new manipulation skills from unstructured video data, moving beyond static skill libraries. This system achieved an average success rate of 0.41 on novel tasks in simulation and 0.73 in real-world settings, demonstrating significant improvements over existing baselines.

title: Uni-Skill: Building Self-Evolving Skill Repository for Generalizable Robotic Manipulation

abstract: LM-based agents excel when given high-level action APIs but struggle to ground language into low-level control. Prior work has LLMs generate skills or reward functions for RL, but these one-shot approaches lack feedback to correct specification errors. We introduce SCALAR, a bidirectional framework coupling LLM planning with RL through a learned skill library. The LLM proposes skills with preconditions and effects; RL trains policies for each skill and feeds back execution results to iteratively refine specifications, improving robustness to initial errors. Pivotal Trajectory Analysis corrects LLM priors by analyzing RL trajectories; Frontier Checkpointing optionally saves environment states at skill boundaries to improve sample efficiency. On Craftax, SCALAR achieves 88.2% diamond collection, a 1.9x improvement over the best baseline, and reaches the Gnomish Mines 9.1% of the time where prior methods fail entirely.

keyInsights: Bidirectional feedback between LLM planning and RL execution enables iterative refinement of skill specifications, correcting initial LLM hypotheses and improving robustness to environmental changes.
Decomposing complex tasks into composable "skills" with focused reward functions makes long-horizon, sparse-reward RL tasks tractable.
Efficient training strategies, like Frontier Checkpointing, are essential for deep prerequisite chains, allowing more training frames to be dedicated to the target skill.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to translate high-level symbolic plans into fine-grained, low-level motor control for direct environmental interaction.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) faces intractable exploration and sparse reward problems in long-horizon tasks requiring deep prerequisite chains.
Existing LLM-RL integration often employs one-shot LLM guidance, lacking feedback mechanisms to refine initial, potentially incorrect, skill specifications or reward functions.
results: Demonstrated 88.2% diamond collection in Craftax-Classic, representing a 1.9x improvement over the best monolithic RL baseline (46.9%).
Achieved a 9.1% success rate on the 'Enter Gnomish Mines' task in Craftax, a complex challenge where other advanced baselines failed to achieve any success.

Trajectory analysis proved critical, boosting diamond collection from 67% to 88% and enabling adaptation to modified crafting recipes and previously missed prerequisites, recovering performance from 5.4% to 86.1% in one adaptation scenario.
solution: Develops SCALAR, a bidirectional LLM-RL framework that iteratively connects symbolic planning from LLMs with deep reinforcement learning for skill acquisition.
Implements an iterative feedback loop where an LLM proposes abstract skill operators and RL agents learn their low-level execution policies, followed by trajectory analysis.
Utilizes "Frontier Checkpointing" to enhance sample efficiency by resetting the environment to states where target skill preconditions are met, bypassing redundant prerequisite skill execution.
paper_summary.summary: The SCALAR framework enables large language models to acquire low-level control skills for complex environments by integrating LLM-guided symbolic planning with deep reinforcement learning through a self-supervised, iterative feedback loop. This system achieved an 88.2% diamond collection rate in Craftax-Classic, a 1.9x improvement over strong baselines, and successfully completed a challenging 'Enter Gnomish Mines' task where prior methods failed.

title: SCALAR: Learning and Composing Skills through LLM Guided Symbolic Planning and Deep RL Grounding

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly applied to tasks requiring structured reasoning, tool use, and environmental adaptation, such as data manipulation, multistep planning, and computer-use automation. However, despite their versatility, current training paradigms for model weight optimization methods, like PPO and GRPO, remain relatively impractical with their high computational overhead for rollout convergence. In addition, the resulting agent policies are difficult to interpret, adapt, or incrementally improve. To address this, we investigate creating and refining structured memory of experiential learning of an agent from its environment as an alternative route to agent optimization. We introduce BREW (Bootstrapping expeRientially-learned Environmental knoWledge), a framework for agent optimization for downstream tasks via KB construction and refinement. In our formulation, we introduce an effective method for partitioning agent memory for more efficient retrieval and refinement. BREW uses task graders and behavior rubrics to learn insights while leveraging state-space search for ensuring robustness from the noise and non-specificity in natural language. Empirical results on real world, domain-grounded benchmarks -- OSWorld, $\tau^2$Bench, and SpreadsheetBench -- show BREW achieves $10-20\%$ improvement in task precision, $10-15\%$ reduction in API/tool calls leading to faster execution time, all while maintaining computational efficiency on par with base models. Unlike prior work where memory is treated as static context, we establish the KB as a modular and controllable substrate for agent optimization -- an explicit lever for shaping behavior in a transparent, interpretable, and extensible manner.

keyInsights: Optimizing an explicit, structured knowledge base in natural language provides an interpretable and computationally efficient alternative to traditional model weight adjustments for enhancing LLM agent performance.
Partitioning the knowledge base by 'meta concepts' allows for modular updates and context-specific retrieval, facilitating effective learning and adaptation in complex, multi-step tasks.

An Expand-and-Gather MCTS strategy effectively navigates the search space for interdependent knowledge components, improving both the utility and discoverability of learned experiential knowledge for LLM agents.
originalProblem: LLM-based agents often fail to retain and leverage experiential knowledge across task sessions, leading to inefficient, repetitive exploration.
Traditional agent optimization methods, such as model weight adjustments, are computationally intensive, slow to converge, and result in opaque policies that are difficult to interpret or debug.
Existing memory solutions for agents lack modularity, fine-grained retrieval, and transparency, often treating memory as transient context rather than a dynamic knowledge base.
results: BREW consistently yielded 10-20% improvements in task precision and a 10-15% reduction in API/tool calls across OSWorld, τ²Bench, and SpreadsheetBench, with computational efficiency comparable to base LLMs.
The Expand-and-Gather MCTS approach surpassed iterative refinement and greedy search strategies, demonstrating superior performance in optimizing the knowledge base across all evaluated benchmarks.

Qualitative analysis revealed BREW generates semantically coherent and adaptive KBs that capture domain-specific strategies, leading to more precise formula placement (90% of cases in SpreadsheetBench) and reduced reliance on granular UI interactions in computer-use automation.
solution: BREW constructs a partitioned 'Memory KB' consisting of concept-level natural language documents, which are dynamically refined from agent trajectories.
It employs a Reflector Agent to extract insights from evaluated rollouts and an Integrator Agent to incrementally update and refine KB documents based on these insights.

An Expand-and-Gather Monte Carlo Tree Search (EG-MCTS) algorithm optimizes the KB by performing parallel explorations of document search spaces, guided by rewards for task correctness and knowledge retrievability.
paper_summary.summary: The BREW framework from Microsoft enables Large Language Model (LLM) agents to build and refine a structured, natural language Knowledge Base (KB) from their experiences, leading to improved performance. This approach achieves a 10-20% increase in task precision and a 10-15% reduction in API/tool calls across various benchmarks.

title: Improving Language Agents through BREW

abstract:
Humans excel at reusing prior knowledge to address new challenges and
developing skills while solving problems. This paradigm becomes increasingly
popular in the development of autonomous agents, as it develops systems that
can self-evolve in response to new challenges like human beings. However,
previous methods suffer from limited training efficiency when expanding new
skills and fail to fully leverage prior knowledge to facilitate new task
learning. In this paper, we propose Parametric Skill Expansion and Composition
(PSEC), a new framework designed to iteratively evolve the agents' capabilities
and efficiently address new challenges by maintaining a manageable skill
library. This library can progressively integrate skill primitives as
plug-and-play Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules in parameter-efficient
finetuning, facilitating efficient and flexible skill expansion. This structure
also enables the direct skill compositions in parameter space by merging LoRA
modules that encode different skills, leveraging shared information across
skills to effectively program new skills. Based on this, we propose a
context-aware module to dynamically activate different skills to
collaboratively handle new tasks. Empowering diverse applications including
multi-objective composition, dynamics shift, and continual policy shift, the
results on D4RL, DSRL benchmarks, and the DeepMind Control Suite show that PSEC
exhibits superior capacity to leverage prior knowledge to efficiently tackle
new challenges, as well as expand its skill libraries to evolve the
capabilities. Project website: this https URL

keyInsights: Composing skills directly in the neural network's parameter space (early fusion) is more effective than combining them at the action or noise level.
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules provide an efficient and isolated way to represent individual skills, enabling their direct interpolation and composition.
Dynamic, context-aware blending of skills based on the current state is essential for agents to adapt to complex and multi-objective tasks.
originalProblem: Autonomous agents typically learn each new task from scratch, leading to high sample and computational inefficiency.
Existing methods struggle with catastrophic forgetting when agents continually acquire new skills.
Prior skill composition approaches often use simplistic skill representations or fixed combination weights, limiting expressiveness and adaptability to dynamic environments.
results: Achieved superior performance in multi-objective composition benchmarks (DSRL), effectively balancing conflicting objectives like reward and safety, outperforming state-of-the-art safe offline RL methods.

Demonstrated high sample and training efficiency in continual policy learning, acquiring new skills with significantly less data (e.g., 10 trajectories) and showing improved performance as the skill library grows.
Enabled robust transfer learning and rapid adaptation to dynamics shifts with limited data, surpassing various offline RL and sample-efficient baselines.
solution: Models policies using expressive diffusion models for diverse behavior representation.
Employs Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) to efficiently encode each new skill as a low-rank module, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.
Introduces a context-aware modular network that dynamically blends these LoRA modules directly in the policy's parameter space based on the current state.
paper_summary.summary: PSEC is a framework that allows autonomous agents to efficiently acquire new skills and compose existing ones by representing policies with diffusion models, encoding skills as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules, and dynamically blending these modules in parameter space via a context-aware network. This method demonstrates superior performance and sample efficiency across tasks involving multi-objective control, continual policy learning, and adaptation to dynamics shifts compared to various baselines.

title: Skill Expansion and Composition in Parameter Space

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) based agents are powerful yet fundamentally static after deployment, lacking the ability to autonomously expand capabilities, generate new tools, or evolve their reasoning. This work introduces a hierarchical self-evolving multi-agent framework that integrates a Base LLM, an operational SLM agent, a Code-Generation LLM, and a Teacher-LLM to enable continuous adaptation. The workflow begins with the agent attempting a task using reasoning and existing tools; if unsuccessful, it escalates to tool synthesis through the Code-Gen LLM, and when failures persist, it triggers an evolution phase using Curriculum Learning (CL), Reward-Based Learning (RL), or Genetic Algorithm (GA) evolution. Using the TaskCraft dataset rich in hierarchical tasks, tool-use traces, and difficulty scaling we evaluate these paradigms. CL delivers fast recovery and strong generalization, RL excels on high-difficulty tasks, and GA offers high behavioral diversity. Across all settings, evolved agents outperform their originals, demonstrating robust, autonomous, self-improving agentic evolution.

keyInsights: Integrating dynamic tool generation and adaptive learning paradigms into a hierarchical multi-agent system provides a pragmatic approach to overcoming the staticity of current LLM agents and advancing towards Artificial General Intelligence.

A multi-faceted approach, leveraging the distinct strengths of Curriculum Learning for efficiency and generalization, Reward-Based Learning for high-difficulty task mastery, and Genetic Algorithms for exploration and diversity, yields comprehensive self-improvement.
Structured feedback loops, verifiable performance metrics, and systematic agent lifecycle management are crucial for enabling scalable, robust, and continuously improving autonomous AI systems.
originalProblem: Existing Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents are static after deployment, operating with fixed architectures, prompts, and workflows.
This static nature severely limits their ability to adapt to novel tasks, dynamic environments, or evolving knowledge distributions.
Current agents lack mechanisms for autonomous capability expansion, reasoning evolution, and continuous improvement, hindering their long-term autonomy and robustness.
results: Evolved agents across all learning paradigms (Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, Genetic Algorithms) consistently and significantly outperformed their original, static counterparts in task success rates on the TaskCraft dataset.
Curriculum Learning demonstrated the highest overall learning efficiency, fastest convergence, and strongest generalization across varying task difficulties, showing the most efficient tool usage.

Reward-Based Learning achieved superior performance on high-difficulty tasks, and Genetic Algorithms exhibited the greatest behavioral diversity and occasional large, rapid performance jumps across generations.
solution: A hierarchical multi-agent framework orchestrates a Base LLM, Operational Agent, Code-Generation LLM, and Teacher-LLM for continuous adaptation and improvement.
The system implements an escalation model: agents first attempt a task, then a Code-Generation LLM synthesizes new tools if needed, and finally, an evolution phase is triggered if failures persist.

The evolution phase utilizes Curriculum Learning, Reward-Based Learning, or Genetic Algorithms to train cloned agents, which are then rigorously evaluated and promoted to replace predecessors if performance improves significantly.
paper_summary.summary: Rastrai researchers developed a hierarchical framework for self-evolving multi-agent Large Language Model systems, enabling agents to autonomously adapt, generate new tools, and refine reasoning. Evaluations using the TaskCraft dataset showed that agents evolved through curriculum learning, reward-based learning, or genetic algorithms consistently surpassed static agents in task success rates, tool efficiency, and overall performance.

title: Towards AGI A Pragmatic Approach Towards Self Evolving Agent

abstract:
Code efficiency is a fundamental aspect of software quality, yet how to harness large language models (LLMs) to optimize programs remains challenging. Prior approaches have sought for one-shot rewriting, retrieved exemplars, or prompt-based search, but they do not explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge, which limits generalization beyond individual instances.
In this paper, we present EffiSkill, a framework for code-efficiency optimization that builds a portable optimization toolbox for LLM-based agents. The key idea is to model recurring slow-to-fast transformations as reusable agent skills that capture both concrete transformation mechanisms and higher-level optimization strategies. EffiSkill adopts a two-stage design: Stage I mines Operator and Meta Skills from large-scale slow/fast program pairs to build a skill library; Stage II applies this library to unseen programs through execution-free diagnosis, skill retrieval, plan composition, and candidate generation, without runtime feedback.
Results on EffiBench-X show that EffiSkill achieves higher optimization success rates, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.69 to 12.52 percentage points across model and language settings. These findings suggest that mechanism-level skill reuse provides a useful foundation for execution-free code optimization, and that the resulting skill library can serve as a reusable resource for broader agent workflows.

keyInsights: Explicitly distilling recurring transformation patterns into a structured, portable library of "agent skills" allows LLMs to apply abstract optimization principles rather than merely imitating surface-level code patterns.
A skill-guided diagnosis, retrieval, and plan composition pipeline can effectively enable execution-free code optimization, addressing practical limitations of online feedback.
Diversified plan exploration and the accurate retrieval of relevant skills are crucial components for discovering high-quality, functionally correct, and measurably faster code.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based code optimization methods often operate at an instance-specific level, struggling with multi-step reasoning and failing to explicitly distill reusable optimization knowledge.
Prior data-driven approaches frequently rely on iterative execution feedback during the optimization process, which is often unavailable or costly in real-world software engineering scenarios.
Traditional code optimization relies on manually designed rules and expert-driven techniques, which typically offer limited coverage and require substantial human effort.
results: EffiSkill consistently achieved higher optimization success rates, outperforming the strongest baselines by up to +12.52 percentage points for OPT@8 on EffiBench-X across Python and C++ using GPT-5-mini and Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B-Instruct.

Ablation studies confirmed that each component of EffiSkill's inference stage (skill retrieval, relevant skill selection, and multi-plan composition) significantly contributed to its overall performance.

The learned skill library consists of 29 compact and diverse Operator Skills, categorized into five semantic families, demonstrating a core set of frequently applicable skills that enable the composition of effective optimization "recipes."
solution: EffiSkill employs a two-stage design: offline skill mining, where recurrent slow-to-fast program transformations are distilled into structured "agent skills," and skill-guided optimization during inference.

It formalizes two types of agent skills: "Operator Skills" that encode concrete transformation mechanisms (e.g., algorithm replacement), and "Meta Skills" that capture higher-level control logic for orchestrating optimization.

During inference, the framework utilizes Meta Skills for diagnosis, retrieves relevant Operator Skills, composes diverse optimization plans, and generates optimized code candidates without execution feedback.
paper_summary.summary: EffiSkill, a framework developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, University of California, Berkeley, and other institutions, enables Large Language Models to optimize code efficiency by distilling and reusing optimization knowledge as agent skills. It achieved up to +12.52 percentage points higher OPT@8 success rates compared to state-of-the-art baselines on the EffiBench-X benchmark for Python and C++, enabling execution-free code improvement.

title: EffiSkill: Agent Skill Based Automated Code Efficiency Optimization

abstract: With the growing adoption of large language model agents in persistent real-world roles, they naturally encounter continuous streams of tasks. A key limitation, however, is their failure to learn from the accumulated interaction history, forcing them to discard valuable insights and repeat past errors. We propose ReasoningBank, a novel memory framework that distills generalizable reasoning strategies from an agent's self-judged successful and failed experiences. At test time, an agent retrieves relevant memories from ReasoningBank to inform its interaction and then integrates new learnings back, enabling it to become more capable over time. Building on this powerful experience learner, we further introduce memory-aware test-time scaling (MaTTS), which accelerates and diversifies this learning process by scaling up the agent's interaction experience. By allocating more compute to each task, the agent generates abundant, diverse experiences that provide rich contrastive signals for synthesizing higher-quality memory. The better memory in turn guides more effective scaling, establishing a powerful synergy between memory and test-time scaling. Across web browsing and software engineering benchmarks, ReasoningBank consistently outperforms existing memory mechanisms that store raw trajectories or only successful task routines, improving both effectiveness and efficiency; MaTTS further amplifies these gains. These findings establish memory-driven experience scaling as a new scaling dimension, enabling agents to self-evolve with emergent behaviors naturally arise.
title: ReasoningBank: Scaling Agent Self-Evolving with Reasoning Memory

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously. Our code can be found in this https URL.

keyInsights: Polymorphic abstraction enables the creation of generalizable skills that are reusable across diverse web environments, moving beyond over-specialized approaches.
Structuring skill learning through a hierarchical, abstract-to-concrete approach accelerates skill acquisition on unseen websites by providing focused exploration goals.
Effective skill induction, which emphasizes reusability and compositionality, directly correlates with improved task efficiency (fewer steps) and overall task success rates.
originalProblem: Existing LLM-based web agents acquire skills that are often over-specialized, failing to generalize across diverse websites or even varied tasks within the same functional domain.
Current skill learning methods produce skills tied to specific UI elements, hindering adaptation to new web environments and limiting effective continual learning.
Evaluation metrics primarily focus on final task success, failing to adequately diagnose skill transfer failures or quantify the reusability and compositional value of learned skills.
results: PolySkill consistently outperformed baselines like ASI across Mind2Web and WebArena benchmarks, achieving up to a 9.4% higher success rate on Mind2Web and a 4.9% overall performance advantage over ASI on WebArena in continual learning scenarios.

The framework demonstrated significantly improved skill generalizability, with 73% of learned skills transferring to unseen websites, a substantial increase from less than 31% for prior methods, and reduced operational steps by over 20%.

PolySkill effectively mitigated catastrophic forgetting in continual learning settings and enabled autonomous skill acquisition in task-free explorative environments, achieving 43.1% success on held-out shopping benchmarks and 66.2% on GitLab, and 84% on GitHub.
