solution: Introduces the PolySkill framework, which leverages polymorphic abstraction from software engineering to decouple a skill's abstract goal from its site-specific implementation.

Establishes a domain-driven skill hierarchy with abstract classes defining high-level goals and method signatures, and concrete subclasses providing distinct, website-specific implementations for these methods.

Guides the LLM-based agent to first induce abstract class interfaces for a new domain and then define concrete skill implementations within site-specific classes that inherit from these abstract blueprints.
paper_summary.summary: PolySkill is a framework that enhances the generalizability of skills for LLM-based web agents by decoupling abstract goals from concrete, site-specific implementations. The framework achieves up to a 9.4% increase in task success rate on Mind2Web benchmarks and improves skill transfer to unseen websites by 73% compared to previous methods, while reducing operational steps by over 20%.

title: PolySkill: Learning Generalizable Skills Through Polymorphic Abstraction

abstract: Large Language Model agents face fundamental challenges in adapting to novel tasks due to limitations in tool availability and experience reuse. Existing approaches either rely on predefined tools with limited coverage or build tools from scratch without leveraging past experiences, leading to inefficient exploration and suboptimal performance. We introduce SMITH (Shared Memory Integrated Tool Hub), a unified cognitive architecture that seamlessly integrates dynamic tool creation with cross-task experience sharing through hierarchical memory organization. SMITH organizes agent memory into procedural, semantic, and episodic components, enabling systematic capability expansion while preserving successful execution patterns. Our approach formalizes tool creation as iterative code generation within controlled sandbox environments and experience sharing through episodic memory retrieval with semantic similarity matching. We further propose a curriculum learning strategy based on agent-ensemble difficulty re-estimation. Extensive experiments on the GAIA benchmark demonstrate SMITH's effectiveness, achieving 81.8% Pass@1 accuracy and outperforming state-of-the-art baselines including Alita (75.2%) and Memento (70.9%). Our work establishes a foundation for building truly adaptive agents that continuously evolve their capabilities through principled integration of tool creation and experience accumulation.

keyInsights: Principled integration of dynamic tool creation and cross-task experience sharing within a unified cognitive architecture leads to higher-performing and more adaptive AI agents.
A hierarchical memory system effectively organizes an agent's knowledge, enabling systematic capability expansion and the preservation of successful execution patterns across diverse tasks.
A model-based curriculum learning strategy with agent-ensemble difficulty re-estimation significantly optimizes learning efficiency and enhances cross-task experience transfer.
originalProblem: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are constrained by limited tool availability, often relying on predefined sets or generating new tools from scratch without reuse.
Current agents struggle to effectively reuse past successful problem-solving experiences, hindering cumulative learning across new but semantically similar tasks.
Existing research approaches for tool creation and experience sharing are fragmented, preventing the development of truly adaptive and continuously learning AI agents.
results: SMITH achieved a state-of-the-art 81.8% Pass@1 accuracy on the GAIA validation set, surpassing leading tool creation methods (Alita) by 6.6% and experience sharing methods (Memento) by 10.9%.
Multi-path sampling combined with an LLM-as-a-judge consensus improved Pass@1 accuracy by 3.0% over the best individual base model, demonstrating enhanced reliability.
Ablation studies showed that the curriculum learning component, based on agent-specific difficulty re-estimation, contributed a substantial 10.3% increase in Pass@1 accuracy.
solution: SMITH (Shared Memory Integrated Tool Hub) introduces a unified cognitive architecture that intrinsically links dynamic tool creation with cross-task experience sharing.

The system employs a hierarchical memory organization comprising procedural, semantic, and episodic components to manage foundational knowledge, external expertise, and online task execution experiences.
It formalizes iterative tool creation as a code generation and refinement process within a sandbox, alongside an episodic memory framework for experience sharing based on semantic task similarity.
paper_summary.summary: A cognitive memory architecture unifies dynamic tool creation with cross-task experience sharing in Large Language Model agents. The proposed SMITH framework achieves an 81.8% Pass@1 accuracy on the GAIA benchmark, demonstrating enhanced adaptability and efficient learning across diverse tasks.

title: Unifying Dynamic Tool Creation and Cross-Task Experience Sharing through Cognitive Memory Architecture

abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for complex real-world tasks, yet existing systems often focus on isolated improvements without a unifying design for robustness and adaptability. We propose a generalist agent architecture that integrates three core components: a collective multi-agent framework combining planning and execution agents with critic model voting, a hierarchical memory system spanning working, semantic, and procedural layers, and a refined tool suite for search, code execution, and multimodal parsing. Evaluated on a comprehensive benchmark, our framework consistently outperforms open-source baselines and approaches the performance of proprietary systems. These results demonstrate the importance of system-level integration and highlight a path toward scalable, resilient, and adaptive AI assistants capable of operating across diverse domains and tasks.

keyInsights: A unified, system-level integration of complementary agent paradigms, hierarchical memory structures, and a refined tool suite is more effective for building robust generalist agents than isolated component improvements.

Balancing the deterministic reliability of Plan–Execute agents with the dynamic adaptability of ReAct agents through ensemble decision-making (critic model) can achieve both stability and flexibility for complex tasks.

Hierarchical memory, particularly dynamically adjusted procedural memory, is crucial for maintaining agent identity, leveraging past knowledge, and adapting strategies over extended, multi-turn interactions.
originalProblem: Existing AI agent systems frequently exhibit brittleness and instability, often struggling with real-world uncertainty and over-reliance on hand-crafted workflows.
Current approaches tend to focus on isolated improvements (e.g., tools, prompts) without a unifying, system-level framework to ensure overall robustness, adaptability, and reproducibility.
There is a performance gap between proprietary and open-source generalist agents on demanding benchmarks that evaluate multimodal reasoning and execution.
results: The framework achieved 75.2 Pass@1 and 82.4 Pass@3 on the GAIA validation set, and 67.1 Pass@1 on the public GAIA leaderboard, demonstrating performance competitive with or exceeding leading proprietary systems.

The proposed fusion method, combining single and multiple agent architectures with a critic model, yielded the highest performance (75.2 Pass@1 average), empirically validating the benefit of diverse reasoning paradigms.

Performance was strongly influenced by the choice of the underlying LLM (Claude-4-sonnet scored highest at 75.2) and search engine (Google Search led with 75.2, significantly outperforming Bing and DuckDuckGo).
solution: An ensemble of heterogeneous agents combines a Plan–Execute paradigm (Supervisor Agent) for global coherence with multiple ReAct paradigm agents (Single Agents) for dynamic adaptability, coordinated by a Critic Model using posterior voting.

A hierarchical memory system, comprising working, semantic, and procedural layers, provides long-horizon continuity by managing immediate context, distilling past experiences, and dynamically adjusting operational guidelines.

A curated and statistically validated tool suite for search, secure code execution, and multimodal parsing (17 specialized interpreters) provides robust factual acquisition and interaction with the digital world through auditable interfaces.
paper_summary.summary: The JoyAgent-JDGenie framework, developed by the JINGDONG CHO-EI Team and Tongji University, provides a generalist agent architecture that integrates heterogeneous agents, a hierarchical memory system, and a refined tool suite. This system achieved 75.2 Pass@1 on the GAIA validation set, outperforming open-source baselines and closely approaching proprietary system capabilities.

title: JoyAgent-JDGenie: Technical Report on the GAIA

abstract: Training large language model (LLM) agents to acquire necessary skills and perform diverse tasks within an environment is gaining interest as a means to enable open-endedness. However, creating the training dataset for their skill acquisition faces several challenges. Manual trajectory collection requires significant human effort. Another approach, where LLMs directly propose tasks to learn, is often invalid, as the LLMs lack knowledge of which tasks are actually feasible. Moreover, the generated data may not provide a meaningful learning signal, as agents often already perform well on the proposed tasks. To address this, we propose a novel automatic skill discovery framework EXIF for LLM-powered agents, designed to improve the feasibility of generated target behaviors while accounting for the agents' capabilities. Our method adopts an exploration-first strategy by employing an exploration agent (Alice) to train the target agent (Bob) to learn essential skills in the environment. Specifically, Alice first interacts with the environment to retrospectively generate a feasible, environment-grounded skill dataset, which is then used to train Bob. Crucially, we incorporate an iterative feedback loop, where Alice evaluates Bob's performance to identify areas for improvement. This feedback then guides Alice's next round of exploration, forming a closed-loop data generation process. Experiments on Webshop and Crafter demonstrate EXIF's ability to effectively discover meaningful skills and iteratively expand the capabilities of the trained agent without any human intervention, achieving substantial performance improvements. Interestingly, we observe that setting Alice to the same model as Bob also notably improves performance, demonstrating EXIF's potential for building a self-evolving system.

keyInsights: An "exploration-first" approach is crucial for generating feasible and environmentally grounded tasks, significantly outperforming "proposal-first" methods in terms of task validity.
Iterative feedback is essential for continuous performance improvement, guiding the exploration agent to generate data that specifically addresses the learning agent's evolving needs and weaknesses.

The framework facilitates the emergence of self-evolving agent systems, demonstrating substantial performance gains even when the same, smaller LLM acts as both the explorer (Alice) and the learner (Bob).
originalProblem: Manual creation of instruction-trajectory datasets for training LLM agents is costly and does not scale.
Existing LLM-based synthetic data generation methods often propose infeasible or irrelevant tasks due to a lack of environmental grounding.
LLM agents frequently struggle with continuous, autonomous skill acquisition and expansion in open-ended environments without significant human intervention.
results: EXIF significantly improved the performance of smaller LLMs (e.g., Llama3.1-8B, Qwen2.5-7B) in Webshop and Crafter, with Llama3.1-8B's reward increasing from 2.0 to 53.7 in Webshop.
Exploration-first strategies achieved high task feasibility (e.g., 88.0% in Webshop, 93.0% in Crafter) compared to "proposal-first" methods (22.0% in Webshop, 45.0% in Crafter).
The system showed consistent iterative performance gains and refined skill acquisition, with feedback guiding Alice towards generating increasingly complex and targeted skills over iterations.
solution: Introduces EXploration and Iterative Feedback (EXIF), an iterative framework employing two LLM agents: Alice (exploration and instruction generation) and Bob (task performing).
Alice uses an "exploration-first" strategy to interact with the environment, generating feasible action trajectories from which natural language instructions are then retrospectively created.
An iterative feedback loop allows Alice to analyze Bob's performance, generate targeted natural language feedback on deficiencies, and guide subsequent exploration for more relevant data generation.
paper_summary.summary: A framework for automated skill discovery in language agents, EXIF, utilizes an exploration-first strategy and iterative feedback to enable continuous learning and expansion of capabilities in complex environments. It demonstrably improves the performance of target LLMs in environments like Webshop and Crafter, even when using the same smaller model for both exploration and learning.

title: Automated Skill Discovery for Language Agents through Exploration and Iterative Feedback

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown strong results on multi-turn tool-use tasks, yet they operate in isolation during training, failing to leverage experiences accumulated across episodes. Existing experience-augmented methods address this by organizing trajectories into retrievable libraries, but they retrieve experiences only once based on the initial task description and hold them constant throughout the episode. In multi-turn settings where observations change at every step, this static retrieval becomes increasingly mismatched as episodes progress. We propose SLEA-RL (Step-Level Experience-Augmented Reinforcement Learning), a framework that retrieves relevant experiences at each decision step conditioned on the current observation. SLEA-RL operates through three components: (i) step-level observation clustering that groups structurally equivalent environmental states for efficient cluster-indexed retrieval; (ii) a self-evolving experience library that distills successful strategies and failure patterns through score-based admission and rate-limited extraction; and (iii) policy optimization with step-level credit assignment for fine-grained advantage estimation across multi-turn episodes. The experience library evolves alongside the policy through semantic analysis rather than gradient updates. Experiments on long-horizon multi-turn agent benchmarks demonstrate that SLEA-RL achieves superior performance compared to various reinforcement learning baselines.

keyInsights: Dynamically retrieving experiences at the step level, conditioned on current observations, significantly outperforms static task-level retrieval by providing more relevant and timely guidance in evolving multi-turn environments.

Combining episode-level and step-level advantage calculations provides more precise credit assignment, enabling the agent's policy to learn more effectively from both overall outcomes and specific intermediate actions.

A self-evolving, quality-controlled experience library allows LLM agents to continuously accumulate and refine interpretable knowledge, leading to faster convergence and higher asymptotic performance compared to standard episodic learning.
originalProblem: LLM agents typically operate in isolation during training, failing to reuse past experiences, which leads to inefficient learning and repeated mistakes in multi-turn tasks.

Existing experience-augmented methods for LLM agents often rely on static, task-level experience retrieval, leading to "staleness" as environmental observations dynamically evolve across multiple turns.

Sparse, terminal rewards in multi-turn environments make fine-grained credit assignment difficult, hindering agents from discerning which specific intermediate actions contributed to overall success or failure.
results: SLEA-RL achieved an average success rate of 93.5% on ALFWorld and 76.3% (87.6% score) on WebShop, outperforming leading RL baselines like GiGPO (90.8% and 72.8% success, respectively).

Demonstrated substantial improvements on multi-hop search-augmented QA tasks, achieving 77.2% on MuSiQue compared to IGPO's 31.4%, and showed robust generalization to out-of-domain benchmarks like TriviaQA (81.8%).

Ablation studies confirmed that both step-level experience retrieval and step-level advantage components are crucial, with their removal leading to noticeable performance drops, and the framework showed faster convergence and more efficient task completion.
solution: A self-evolving experience library (E) is maintained, partitioned into strategy (E+) and warning (E-) zones, and updated through quality-controlled semantic analysis of successful and failed trajectories.
Step-level observation clustering and retrieval dynamically fetch relevant experiences from cluster-indexed pools at each decision step, ensuring guidance is conditioned on the current observation.

Policy optimization extends GRPO by incorporating multi-level credit assignment, combining episode-level and step-level advantages to provide fine-grained feedback for policy updates, especially in long multi-turn episodes.
paper_summary.summary: SLEA-RL enhances LLM agents for complex multi-turn tasks by integrating step-level experience retrieval, fine-grained credit assignment, and a self-evolving experience library. This framework achieved 93.5% average success on ALFWorld and 76.3% on WebShop, surpassing prior RL and experience-augmented methods, and demonstrated robust generalization on search-augmented QA tasks.

title: SLEA-RL: Step-Level Experience Augmented Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Agentic Training

abstract: Skill Incremental Learning (SIL) is the process by which an embodied agent expands and refines its skill set over time by leveraging experience gained through interaction with its environment or by the integration of additional data. SIL facilitates efficient acquisition of hierarchical policies grounded in reusable skills for downstream tasks. However, as the skill repertoire evolves, it can disrupt compatibility with existing skill-based policies, limiting their reusability and generalization. In this work, we propose SIL-C, a novel framework that ensures skill-policy compatibility, allowing improvements in incrementally learned skills to enhance the performance of downstream policies without requiring policy re-training or structural adaptation. SIL-C employs a bilateral lazy learning-based mapping technique to dynamically align the subtask space referenced by policies with the skill space decoded into agent behaviors. This enables each subtask, derived from the policy's decomposition of a complex task, to be executed by selecting an appropriate skill based on trajectory distribution similarity. We evaluate SIL-C across diverse SIL scenarios and demonstrate that it maintains compatibility between evolving skills and downstream policies while ensuring efficiency throughout the learning process.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin

keyInsights: A 'lazy learning interface' can effectively mediate between an evolving skill library and fixed high-level policies, providing both forward and backward compatibility without policy re-training.

Instance-based classification using multimodal Gaussian prototypes and Mahalanobis distance enables robust skill validation, out-of-distribution detection, and effective skill hooking for dynamic adaptation.
Decoupling skill evolution from the re-training burden of downstream policies through such an interface allows for more modular, scalable, and sample-efficient lifelong learning in embodied agents.
originalProblem: Maintaining compatibility between an agent's evolving skill set and its pre-existing hierarchical downstream policies in Skill Incremental Learning (SIL).
Updates to low-level skills can render previously learned high-level policies incompatible, limiting their reusability and generalization, and often necessitating expensive re-training.
Existing SIL methods typically assume synchronous updates or lack robust mechanisms for long-term skill reuse and accumulation without requiring policy re-training.
results: SIL-C consistently achieved the highest Area Under the Curve (AUC) across various SIL scenarios, improving backward skill compatibility (BwSC) by 18.6 to 42.5 percentage points in normalized reward in the Franka Kitchen environment.

Demonstrated superior sample efficiency, outperforming baselines in few-shot imitation learning (e.g., improving overall AUC from 31.7% to 56.5% in a 1-shot setting) and showing robustness to sparse supervision.

Exhibited high modularity, enhancing initial policy performance across different environments and underlying skill learning algorithms, and maintained positive backward transfer even under extreme observation noise.
solution: Proposed SIL-C, a hierarchical architecture integrating a novel 'lazy learning interface' to dynamically connect high-level policies and low-level skill decoders.
Implemented the interface as a two-stage, instance-based classifier with bilateral mechanisms for skill validation and intelligent skill hooking, operating across subtask and skill spaces.

Employed append-only prototype memories for interface modules and behavior cloning for policy learning, ensuring non-destructive skill updates and dynamic skill-policy alignment during inference without policy re-training.
paper_summary.summary: A framework named SIL-C is presented, designed to maintain skill-policy compatibility in Skill Incremental Learning for embodied agents by enabling continuous skill acquisition and refinement without requiring re-training of existing hierarchical policies. It improved backward skill compatibility by an average of 18.6 to 42.5 percentage points in normalized reward in the Franka Kitchen environment and demonstrated superior sample efficiency in few-shot imitation learning settings.

title: Policy Compatible Skill Incremental Learning via Lazy Learning Interface

abstract: Large language model agents often fail to accumulate knowledge from experience, treating each task as an independent challenge. Recent methods extract experience as flattened textual knowledge, which cannot capture procedural logic of complex subtasks. They also lack maintenance mechanisms, causing repository degradation as experience accumulates. We introduce AutoRefine, a framework that extracts and maintains dual-form Experience Patterns from agent execution histories. For procedural subtasks, we extract specialized subagents with independent reasoning and memory. For static knowledge, we extract skill patterns as guidelines or code snippets. A continuous maintenance mechanism scores, prunes, and merges patterns to prevent repository degradation. Evaluated on ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, and TravelPlanner, AutoRefine achieves 98.4%, 70.4%, and 27.1% respectively, with 20-73% step reductions. On TravelPlanner, automatic extraction exceeds manually designed systems (27.1% vs 12.1%), demonstrating its ability to capture procedural coordination.

keyInsights: The dual-form pattern representation, particularly Subagent Patterns, is crucial for effectively encapsulating and reusing complex procedural logic, leading to improved performance on multi-step tasks.
Batch-level pattern extraction, by comparing successful and failed trajectories, is essential for identifying generalizable strategies and preventing overfitting to specific task details.

Continuous maintenance (scoring, pruning, and merging) is indispensable for preventing repository degradation, ensuring high pattern utilization, and maintaining the efficiency and quality of learned experience.
originalProblem: LLM agents often treat each task as an isolated problem, failing to accumulate and reuse knowledge from past successful executions.
Existing knowledge extraction methods primarily use flattened textual representations, which inadequately capture the complex, stateful procedural logic required for multi-step subtasks.
A lack of continuous experience maintenance mechanisms leads to uncurated, ever-growing knowledge repositories that burden LLM context windows and degrade retrieval quality.
results: AutoRefine achieved higher success rates on all evaluated benchmarks: 98.4% on ALFWorld, 70.4% on ScienceWorld, and 27.1% on TravelPlanner, outperforming strong baselines.
The framework substantially reduced execution steps, with improvements of 20.5% in ALFWorld, 59.0% in ScienceWorld, and 72.8% in TravelPlanner compared to ReAct + Reflexion.

Ablation studies demonstrated that removing Subagent Patterns caused the largest performance drop (e.g., from 35.6% to 13.3% pass rate on TravelPlanner), highlighting their critical role in procedural encapsulation.
solution: AutoRefine introduces a dual-form pattern repository comprising Skill Patterns (natural language guidelines or executable code) for general knowledge and Subagent Patterns (specialized, stateful agents) for complex procedural logic.
It employs a three-stage framework: Task Execution with pattern guidance, Agent-Driven Pattern Extraction from successful and failed trajectories, and continuous Pattern Maintenance.
A utility-based scoring system prunes low-quality patterns and merges redundant ones, ensuring the experience repository remains compact, high-quality, and scalable over time.
paper_summary.summary: AutoRefine introduces a framework for large language model (LLM) agents to continually learn from their experiences by extracting and maintaining dual-form expertise patterns. The system achieved higher success rates and significantly reduced execution steps across multiple benchmarks, including a 72.8% step reduction in TravelPlanner tasks.

title: AutoRefine: From Trajectories to Reusable Expertise for Continual LLM Agent Refinement

abstract: The ability to effectively reuse prior knowledge is a key requirement when building general and flexible Reinforcement Learning (RL) agents. Skill reuse is one of the most common approaches, but current methods have considerable this http URL example, fine-tuning an existing policy frequently fails, as the policy can degrade rapidly early in training. In a similar vein, distillation of expert behavior can lead to poor results when given sub-optimal experts. We compare several common approaches for skill transfer on multiple domains including changes in task and system dynamics. We identify how existing methods can fail and introduce an alternative approach to mitigate these problems. Our approach learns to sequence existing temporally-extended skills for exploration but learns the final policy directly from the raw experience. This conceptual split enables rapid adaptation and thus efficient data collection but without constraining the final this http URL significantly outperforms many classical methods across a suite of evaluation tasks and we use a broad set of ablations to highlight the importance of differentc omponents of our method.
title: SkillS: Adaptive Skill Sequencing for Efficient Temporally-Extended Exploration

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously.
title: PolySkill: Learning Generalizable Skills Through Polymorphic Abstraction

abstract: Autonomous agents powered by large language models (LLMs) have the potential to significantly enhance human productivity by reasoning, using tools, and executing complex tasks in diverse environments. However, current approaches to developing such agents remain costly and inefficient, as they typically require manually constructed task datasets and reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines with extensive random exploration. These limitations lead to prohibitively high data-construction costs, low exploration efficiency, and poor sample utilization. To address these challenges, we present AgentEvolver, a self-evolving agent system that leverages the semantic understanding and reasoning capabilities of LLMs to drive autonomous agent learning. AgentEvolver introduces three synergistic mechanisms: (i) self-questioning, which enables curiosity-driven task generation in novel environments, reducing dependence on handcrafted datasets; (ii) self-navigating, which improves exploration efficiency through experience reuse and hybrid policy guidance; and (iii) self-attributing, which enhances sample efficiency by assigning differentiated rewards to trajectory states and actions based on their contribution. By integrating these mechanisms into a unified framework, AgentEvolver enables scalable, cost-effective, and continual improvement of agent capabilities. Preliminary experiments indicate that AgentEvolver achieves more efficient exploration, better sample utilization, and faster adaptation compared to traditional RL-based baselines.
title: AgentEvolver: Towards Efficient Self-Evolving Agent System

abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown promise in tackling complex tasks by interacting dynamically with the environment. Existing work primarily focuses on behavior cloning from expert demonstrations or preference learning through exploratory trajectory sampling. However, these methods often struggle to address long-horizon tasks, where suboptimal actions accumulate step by step, causing agents to deviate from correct task trajectories. To address this, we highlight the importance of timely calibration and the need to automatically construct calibration trajectories for training agents. We propose Step-Level Trajectory Calibration (STeCa), a novel framework for LLM agent learning. Specifically, STeCa identifies suboptimal actions through a step-level reward comparison during exploration. It constructs calibrated trajectories using LLM-driven reflection, enabling agents to learn from improved decision-making processes. We finally leverage these calibrated trajectories with successful trajectories for reinforced training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that STeCa significantly outperforms existing methods. Further analysis highlights that timely calibration enables agents to complete tasks with greater robustness. Our code and data are available at this https URL.

abstract:
We introduce \emph{Memento-Skills}, a generalist, continually-learnable LLM agent system that functions as an \emph{agent-designing agent}: it autonomously constructs, adapts, and improves task-specific agents through experience. The system is built on a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with \emph{stateful prompts}, where reusable skills (stored as structured markdown files) serve as persistent, evolving memory. These skills encode both behaviour and context, enabling the agent to carry forward knowledge across interactions.
Starting from simple elementary skills (like Web search and terminal operations), the agent continually improves via the \emph{Read--Write Reflective Learning} mechanism introduced in \emph{Memento~2}~\cite{wang2025memento2}. In the \emph{read} phase, a behaviour-trainable skill router selects the most relevant skill conditioned on the current stateful prompt; in the \emph{write} phase, the agent updates and expands its skill library based on new experience. This closed-loop design enables \emph{continual learning without updating LLM parameters}, as all adaptation is realised through the evolution of externalised skills and prompts.
Unlike prior approaches that rely on human-designed agents, Memento-Skills enables a generalist agent to \emph{design agents end-to-end} for new tasks. Through iterative skill generation and refinement, the system progressively improves its own capabilities. Experiments on the \emph{General AI Assistants} benchmark and \emph{Humanity's Last Exam} demonstrate sustained gains, achieving 26.2\% and 116.2\% relative improvements in overall accuracy, respectively. Code is available at this https URL.
title: Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents

The system is built on a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with \emph{stateful prompts}, where reusable skills (stored as structured markdown files) serve as persistent, evolving memory. These skills encode both behaviour and context, enabling the agent to carry forward knowledge across interactions.
Starting from simple elementary skills (like Web search and terminal operations), the agent continually improves via the \emph{Read--Write Reflective Learning} mechanism introduced in \emph{Memento~2}~\cite{wang2025memento2}. In the \emph{read} phase, a behaviour-trainable skill router selects the most relevant skill conditioned on the current stateful prompt; in the \emph{write} phase, the agent updates and expands its skill library based on new experience. This closed-loop design enables \emph{continual learning without updating LLM parameters}, as all adaptation is realised through the evolution of externalised skills and prompts.
Unlike prior approaches that rely on human-designed agents, Memento-Skills enables a generalist agent to \emph{design agents end-to-end} for new tasks. Through iterative skill generation and refinement, the system progressively improves its own capabilities. Experiments on the \emph{General AI Assistants} benchmark and \emph{Humanity's Last Exam} demonstrate sustained gains, achieving 26.2\% and 116.2\% relative improvements in overall accuracy, respectively. Code is available at this https URL.
title: Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents

abstract: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) agents have shown remarkable progress in software issue resolution, leveraging advanced techniques such as multi-agent collaboration and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS). However, current agents act as memoryless explorers - treating each problem separately without retaining or reusing knowledge from previous repair experiences. This leads to redundant exploration of failed trajectories and missed chances to adapt successful issue resolution methods to similar problems. To address this problem, we introduce SWE-Exp, an experience - enhanced approach that distills concise and actionable experience from prior agent trajectories, enabling continuous learning across issues. Our method introduces a multi-faceted experience bank that captures both successful and failed repair attempts. Specifically, it extracts reusable issue resolution knowledge at different levels - from high-level problem comprehension to specific code changes. Experiments show that SWE-Exp achieves state-of-the-art resolution rate (41.6% Pass@1) on SWE-bench-Verified under open-source agent frameworks. Our approach establishes a new paradigm in which automated software engineering agents systematically accumulate and leverage repair expertise, fundamentally shifting from trial-and-error exploration to strategic, experience-driven issue resolution.
title: SWE-Exp: Experience-Driven Software Issue Resolution

abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown stunning results in complex tasks, yet they often operate in isolation, failing to learn from past experiences. Existing memory-based methods primarily store raw trajectories, which are often redundant and noise-heavy. This prevents agents from extracting high-level, reusable behavioral patterns that are essential for generalization. In this paper, we propose SkillRL, a framework that bridges the gap between raw experience and policy improvement through automatic skill discovery and recursive evolution. Our approach introduces an experience-based distillation mechanism to build a hierarchical skill library SkillBank, an adaptive retrieval strategy for general and task-specific heuristics, and a recursive evolution mechanism that allows the skill library to co-evolve with the agent's policy during reinforcement learning. These innovations significantly reduce the token footprint while enhancing reasoning utility. Experimental results on ALFWorld, WebShop and seven search-augmented tasks demonstrate that SkillRL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming strong baselines over 15.3% and maintaining robustness as task complexity increases. Code is available at this this https URL.
title: SkillRL: Evolving Agents via Recursive Skill-Augmented Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are moving beyond static uses and are now powering agents that learn continually during their interaction with external environments. For example, agents can learn reusable skills while navigating web pages or toggling new tools. However, existing methods for skill learning often create skills that are over-specialized to a single website and fail to generalize. We introduce PolySkill, a new framework that enables agents to learn generalizable and compositional skills. The core idea, inspired by polymorphism in software engineering, is to decouple a skill's abstract goal (what it accomplishes) and its concrete implementation (how it is executed). Experiments show that our method (1) improves skill reuse by 1.7x on seen websites and (2) boosts success rates by up to 9.4% on Mind2Web and 13.9% on unseen websites, while reducing steps by over 20%. (3) In self-exploration settings without specified tasks, our framework improves the quality of proposed tasks and enables agents to learn generalizable skills that work across different sites. By enabling the agent to identify and refine its own goals, the PolySkill enhances the agent's ability to learn a better curriculum, leading to the acquisition of more generalizable skills compared to baseline methods. This work provides a practical path toward building agents capable of continual learning in adaptive environments. Our findings show that separating a skill's goal from its execution is a crucial step toward developing autonomous agents that can learn and generalize across the open web continuously.

abstract: With the growing adoption of large language model agents in persistent real-world roles, they naturally encounter continuous streams of tasks. A key limitation, however, is their failure to learn from the accumulated interaction history, forcing them to discard valuable insights and repeat past errors. We propose ReasoningBank, a novel memory framework that distills generalizable reasoning strategies from an agent's self-judged successful and failed experiences. At test time, an agent retrieves relevant memories from ReasoningBank to inform its interaction and then integrates new learnings back, enabling it to become more capable over time. Building on this powerful experience learner, we further introduce memory-aware test-time scaling (MaTTS), which accelerates and diversifies this learning process by scaling up the agent's interaction experience. By allocating more compute to each task, the agent generates abundant, diverse experiences that provide rich contrastive signals for synthesizing higher-quality memory. The better memory in turn guides more effective scaling, establishing a powerful synergy between memory and test-time scaling. Across web browsing and software engineering benchmarks, ReasoningBank consistently outperforms existing memory mechanisms that store raw trajectories or only successful task routines, improving both effectiveness and efficiency; MaTTS further amplifies these gains. These findings establish memory-driven experience scaling as a new scaling dimension, enabling agents to self-evolve with emergent behaviors naturally arise.
title: ReasoningBank: Scaling Agent Self-Evolving with Reasoning Memory

abstract: Procedural memory enables large language model (LLM) agents to internalize "how-to" knowledge, theoretically reducing redundant trial-and-error. However, existing frameworks predominantly suffer from a "passive accumulation" paradigm, treating memory as a static append-only archive. To bridge the gap between static storage and dynamic reasoning, we propose $\textbf{ReMe}$ ($\textit{Remember Me, Refine Me}$), a comprehensive framework for experience-driven agent evolution. ReMe innovates across the memory lifecycle via three mechanisms: 1) $\textit{multi-faceted distillation}$, which extracts fine-grained experiences by recognizing success patterns, analyzing failure triggers and generating comparative insights; 2) $\textit{context-adaptive reuse}$, which tailors historical insights to new contexts via scenario-aware indexing; and 3) $\textit{utility-based refinement}$, which autonomously adds valid memories and prunes outdated ones to maintain a compact, high-quality experience pool. Extensive experiments on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld demonstrate that ReMe establishes a new state-of-the-art in agent memory system. Crucially, we observe a significant memory-scaling effect: Qwen3-8B equipped with ReMe outperforms larger, memoryless Qwen3-14B, suggesting that self-evolving memory provides a computation-efficient pathway for lifelong learning. We release our code and the $\texttt{this http URL}$ dataset to facilitate further research.
title: Remember Me, Refine Me: A Dynamic Procedural Memory Framework for Experience-Driven Agent Evolution

abstract: Humans excel at reusing prior knowledge to address new challenges and developing skills while solving problems. This paradigm becomes increasingly popular in the development of autonomous agents, as it develops systems that can self-evolve in response to new challenges like human beings. However, previous methods suffer from limited training efficiency when expanding new skills and fail to fully leverage prior knowledge to facilitate new task learning. In this paper, we propose Parametric Skill Expansion and Composition (PSEC), a new framework designed to iteratively evolve the agents' capabilities and efficiently address new challenges by maintaining a manageable skill library. This library can progressively integrate skill primitives as plug-and-play Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules in parameter-efficient finetuning, facilitating efficient and flexible skill expansion. This structure also enables the direct skill compositions in parameter space by merging LoRA modules that encode different skills, leveraging shared information across skills to effectively program new skills. Based on this, we propose a context-aware module to dynamically activate different skills to collaboratively handle new tasks. Empowering diverse applications including multi-objective composition, dynamics shift, and continual policy shift, the results on D4RL, DSRL benchmarks, and the DeepMind Control Suite show that PSEC exhibits superior capacity to leverage prior knowledge to efficiently tackle new challenges, as well as expand its skill libraries to evolve the capabilities. Project website: this https URL.
title: Skill Expansion and Composition in Parameter Space

keyInsights: Treating procedural memory as a dynamic, self-evolving cognitive substrate, rather than static storage, is essential for continuous agent improvement.
Active refinement mechanisms, including selective experience addition, failure-aware reflection, and utility-based deletion, are critical for maintaining a high-quality and compact memory pool.
Effective memory systems can enable a "memory-scaling effect," allowing smaller language models to achieve performance comparable to or exceeding larger, memoryless models.
originalProblem: Existing procedural memory frameworks for LLM agents primarily rely on a "passive accumulation" paradigm, treating memory as static storage.
This leads to coarse-grained experiences, storing irrelevant information, and a lack of dynamic adaptation of retrieved memories to new task contexts.
Memory pools degrade over time due to the absence of strategic update or removal mechanisms for outdated or ineffective experiences.
results: ReMe achieved state-of-the-art performance on BFCL-V3 and AppWorld benchmarks, with Qwen3-8B + ReMe (dynamic) demonstrating an 8.83% average gain in Avg@4 and 7.29% in Pass@4 compared to a "No Memory" baseline.
The framework exhibited a "memory-scaling effect," enabling smaller models like Qwen3-8B with ReMe to surpass the performance of a larger, memoryless Qwen3-14B model (e.g., 55.03% Pass@4 vs. 54.65%).

Ablation studies validated the importance of fine-grained, keypoint-level experience extraction and the core refinement components, including selective addition and utility-based deletion, which collectively reduced reasoning and action omission errors.
solution: Introduces ReMe, a dynamic procedural memory framework with three phases: experience acquisition, reuse, and refinement.
Experience acquisition employs multi-faceted distillation from successful, failed, and comparative trajectories, followed by validation and deduplication to generate high-quality experiences.
Experience reuse involves retrieving relevant insights, context-aware reranking, and adaptively rewriting them into task-specific guidance for the agent.
Experience refinement dynamically optimizes the memory pool through selective addition of successful experiences, failure-aware reflection, and utility-based deletion of ineffective entries.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Tongyi Lab at Alibaba Group developed ReMe, a dynamic procedural memory framework that enables large language model agents to continuously learn and adapt from their experiences. It enhances agent performance and offers a computationally efficient pathway for lifelong learning by allowing smaller models to surpass larger memoryless counterparts.

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) based agents excel at diverse tasks, yet they suffer from brittle procedural memory that is manually engineered or entangled in static parameters. In this work, we investigate strategies to endow agents with a learnable, updatable, and lifelong procedural memory. We propose Memp that distills past agent trajectories into both fine-grained, step-by-step instructions and higher-level, script-like abstractions, and explore the impact of different strategies for Build, Retrieval, and Update of procedural memory. Coupled with a dynamic regimen that continuously updates, corrects, and deprecates its contents, this repository evolves in lockstep with new experience. Empirical evaluation on TravelPlanner and ALFWorld shows that as the memory repository is refined, agents achieve steadily higher success rates and greater efficiency on analogous tasks. Moreover, procedural memory built from a stronger model retains its value: migrating the procedural memory to a weaker model can also yield substantial performance gains. Code is available at this https URL.
title: Memp: Exploring Agent Procedural Memory

abstract: Most Large Language Model (LLM) agent memory systems rely on a small set of static, hand-designed operations for extracting memory. These fixed procedures hard-code human priors about what to store and how to revise memory, making them rigid under diverse interaction patterns and inefficient on long histories. To this end, we present \textbf{MemSkill}, which reframes these operations as learnable and evolvable memory skills, structured and reusable routines for extracting, consolidating, and pruning information from interaction traces. Inspired by the design philosophy of agent skills, MemSkill employs a \emph{controller} that learns to select a small set of relevant skills, paired with an LLM-based \emph{executor} that produces skill-guided memories. Beyond learning skill selection, MemSkill introduces a \emph{designer} that periodically reviews hard cases where selected skills yield incorrect or incomplete memories, and evolves the skill set by proposing refinements and new skills. Together, MemSkill forms a closed-loop procedure that improves both the skill-selection policy and the skill set itself. Experiments on LoCoMo, LongMemEval, HotpotQA, and ALFWorld demonstrate that MemSkill improves task performance over strong baselines and generalizes well across settings. Further analyses shed light on how skills evolve, offering insights toward more adaptive, self-evolving memory management for LLM agents.
title: MemSkill: Learning and Evolving Memory Skills for Self-Evolving Agents

abstract: Multimodal LLM agents operating in complex game environments must continually reuse past experience to solve new tasks efficiently. In this work, we propose Echo, a transfer-oriented memory framework that enables agents to derive actionable knowledge from prior interactions rather than treating memory as a passive repository of static records. To make transfer explicit, Echo decomposes reusable knowledge into five dimensions: structure, attribute, process, function, and interaction. This formulation allows the agent to identify recurring patterns shared across different tasks and infer what prior experience remains applicable in new situations. Building on this formulation, Echo leverages In-Context Analogy Learning (ICAL) to retrieve relevant experiences and adapt them to unseen tasks through contextual examples. Experiments in Minecraft show that, under a from-scratch learning setting, Echo achieves a 1.3x to 1.7x speed-up on object-unlocking tasks. Moreover, Echo exhibits a burst-like chain-unlocking phenomenon, rapidly unlocking multiple similar items within a short time interval after acquiring transferable experience. These results suggest that experience transfer is a promising direction for improving the efficiency and adaptability of multimodal LLM agents in complex interactive environments.

keyInsights: Explicit decomposition of knowledge into transfer dimensions significantly improves multi-axis similarity modeling, enhancing task success and interpretability.
Combining structured memory with In-Context Analogy Learning enables effective cross-task and cross-world generalization for MLLM agents.
A consistency self-checking mechanism is crucial for the stability and verifiability of long-horizon planning in open-ended environments.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal LLM agents treat memory as a passive repository, limiting active knowledge transfer across diverse tasks and environments.
Open, interactive environments present complex regularities and structural dependencies, making effective knowledge transfer and generalization challenging for agents.
MLLMs can exhibit instability and hallucination in open-ended scenarios, undermining the reliability and transferability of their generalization performance.
results: Echo achieved a 1.3	imes	extendash{}1.7	imes speed-up in object-unlocking tasks and surpassed baselines in few-shot cross-world learning, reaching up to 92.5% success in some tasks.
In continuous learning, Echo demonstrated sustained growth and reached the highest success rate of 45% by episode 30, outperforming other agents like JARVIS-1 and Voyager.

Ablation studies revealed that removing any single transfer dimension led to notable performance drops, with procedural and attribute axes causing up to 12% and 11% degradation respectively in relevant tasks.
solution: Echo decomposes environmental and experiential knowledge into five explicit transfer dimensions: structural, attribute, procedural, functional, and interaction.
It uses Contextual State Descriptors (CSD) to compress heterogeneous multimodal inputs into unified, comparable semantic snapshots for knowledge transfer.
An In-Context Analogy Learning (ICAL) workflow, leveraging an instruction-tuned MLLM, infers new task plans from retrieved CSD examples and incorporates a consistency self-checking mechanism.
paper_summary.summary: The Echo framework introduces explicit transfer dimensions and in-context analogy learning for multimodal LLM agents, enabling them to derive actionable knowledge from past interactions. This approach improves learning efficiency and cross-task generalization in complex game environments, demonstrating faster object unlocking and higher long-term task success rates compared to existing methods.

title: Experience Transfer for Multimodal LLM Agents in Minecraft Game
